edges.description: Reduced PRDM12 activity impairs the developmental program that normally establishes NTRK1 expression in nociceptor precursors.
Failure of trophic support causes early nociceptor loss during development.
The same pathway also contributes to macrophage antibacterial responses, explaining infection susceptibility in developmental CIP forms.
Early apoptosis yields the characteristic loss of pain-transducing small fibers in developmental CIP.
Structural denervation removes the afferent substrate required for normal pain sensing.
Loss of Nav1.7 function leaves nociceptors present but unable to transmit normal warning signals.
Sustained depolarization prevents normal action-potential generation in nociceptors.
Failure of nociceptor firing abolishes warning pain despite preserved neuronal anatomy.
Reduced macrophage killing capacity predisposes to recurrent deep and skin infections in developmental CIP forms.

nodes.description: Developmental CIP converts trophic failure into structural denervation by early loss of nociceptors.

evidence.reference_title: Understanding the genetic basis of congenital insensitivity to pain.
evidence.snippet: CIP can be clinically divided into two groups: developmental disorders where nociceptors fail to develop or undergo early apoptosis through lack of trophic signals;

nodes.description: Developmental NGF-TRKA CIP forms also weaken macrophage-mediated host defense against Staphylococcus aureus.

evidence.reference_title: Understanding the genetic basis of congenital insensitivity to pain.
evidence.snippet: Increased susceptibility to Staphylococcus aureus infection is a consequence of deficient NGF-TRKA signalling.

nodes.description: In channelopathy-associated CIP, nociceptors are present but electrically unable to generate or propagate appropriate warning signals.

evidence.reference_title: Understanding the genetic basis of congenital insensitivity to pain.
evidence.snippet: Individuals with second type of CIP are born with nociceptors that are unable to be activated by tissue-damage signals,

nodes.description: PRDM12-related CIP begins as a developmental failure to impose nociceptor identity and precursor differentiation.

evidence.reference_title: Understanding the genetic basis of congenital insensitivity to pain.
evidence.snippet: PRDM12 and CLTCL1 are important in the differentiation from a neural crest cell to a precursor sensory neuron.

nodes.description: Both structural denervation and channelopathic firing failure converge on the same final phenotype: absent protective pain responses with severe injury-related morbidity.

evidence.reference_title: Understanding the genetic basis of congenital insensitivity to pain.
evidence.snippet: Congenital insensitivity to pain (CIP) is an extremely rare human phenotype where no pain of any type is experienced during an affected individuals’ lifetime.

evidence.reference_title: A mutation in the nerve growth factor beta gene (NGFB) causes loss of pain perception.
evidence.snippet: Clinically they best fit into HSAN V.

evidence.reference_title: NGF / hereditary sensory and autonomic neuropathy (Strong)
evidence.snippet: NGF | HGNC:7808 | hereditary sensory and autonomic neuropathy | MONDO:0015364 | AR | Strong

nodes.description: NTRK1- and NGF-related developmental CIP disrupts the neurotrophin signaling axis needed for nociceptor genesis, maturation, and survival.

evidence.reference_title: Understanding the genetic basis of congenital insensitivity to pain.
evidence.snippet: The importance of nerve growth factor-tropomyosin receptor kinase A (NGF-TRKA) signalling for nociceptor genesis and subsequent pain sensing.

evidence.reference_title: Congenital insensitivity to pain with Anhidrosis (NTRK1 mutation) and early onset renal disease: clinical report on three sibs with a 25-year follow-up in one of them.
evidence.snippet: Congenital insensitivity to pain with anhidrosis (CIPA) is an autosomal recessive disorder caused by mutations in the neurotrophic tyrosine receptor kinase 1 (NTRK1) gene which encodes the receptor for nerve growth factor (NGF).

nodes.description: SCN9A-associated CIP preserves nociceptors anatomically but disables Nav1.7, preventing normal electrical responses to tissue-damage signals.

evidence.reference_title: Understanding the genetic basis of congenital insensitivity to pain.
evidence.snippet: So, activating mutations in SCN9A produce paroxysmal neuropathic pain, with no other obvious clinical features. And bi-allelic non-functional mutations cause congenital painlessness, with the only other consequence being anosmia.

nodes.description: SCN11A-associated CIP is a paradoxical gain-of-function state in which excessive resting activity of Nav1.9 depolarizes nociceptors and blocks productive action-potential firing.

evidence.reference_title: A de novo gain-of-function mutation in SCN11A causes loss of pain perception.
evidence.snippet: Mutant Nav1.9 channels displayed excessive activity at resting voltages, causing sustained depolarization of nociceptors, impaired generation of action potentials and aberrant synaptic transmission.

evidence.reference_title: Understanding the genetic basis of congenital insensitivity to pain.
evidence.snippet: In 2015 a new HSAN4-like phenotype was reported caused by bi-allelic mutations in PRDM12 (clinically known as HSAN6).

evidence.reference_title: Understanding the genetic basis of congenital insensitivity to pain.
evidence.snippet: The recent finding that PRDM12 activated NTRK1 expression in nociceptor precursors further cemented the absolute importance of NGF-TRKA signalling for nociceptor genesis.

nodes.description: Developmental CIP forms show reduced C-fiber and A-delta-fiber innervation rather than merely an altered pain threshold.

evidence.reference_title: A mutation in the nerve growth factor beta gene (NGFB) causes loss of pain perception.
evidence.snippet: A severe reduction of unmyelinated nerve fibers and a moderate loss of thin myelinated nerve fibers are observed in the patients.

evidence.reference_title: A de novo gain-of-function mutation in SCN11A causes loss of pain perception.
evidence.snippet: Using exome sequencing, we identified a specific de novo missense mutation in SCN11A in individuals with the congenital inability to experience pain who suffer from recurrent tissue damage and severe mutilations.

evidence.reference_title: A de novo gain-of-function mutation in SCN11A causes loss of pain perception.
evidence.snippet: Mutant Nav1.9 channels displayed excessive activity at resting voltages, causing sustained depolarization of nociceptors, impaired generation of action potentials and aberrant synaptic transmission.

evidence.reference_title: Understanding the genetic basis of congenital insensitivity to pain.
evidence.snippet: So, activating mutations in SCN9A produce paroxysmal neuropathic pain, with no other obvious clinical features. And bi-allelic non-functional mutations cause congenital painlessness, with the only other consequence being anosmia.

nodes.description: Developmental CIP forms, but not channelopathy-associated forms, show a distinctive propensity to osteomyelitis, septic arthritis, and skin infection with Staphylococcus aureus.

evidence.reference_title: Understanding the genetic basis of congenital insensitivity to pain.
evidence.snippet: Distinctive to HSANs is a significant susceptibility to Staphylococcus aureus infections manifest as grumbling skin infections, osteomyelitis and septic arthritis.
